event: San (Bushmen) hunter-gatherers inhabit the region, leaving behind extensive rock art across the highlands and caves.
Stone Age San communities flourish in the mountainous terrain, developing the rich rock-painting tradition later found at sites like Ha Baroana.
Bantu-speaking peoples begin migrating into southern Africa, gradually introducing iron-working and agriculture to the region.
Sotho-Tswana speaking groups settle on the highveld and foothills, establishing farming and cattle-herding communities.
Various Sotho clans, including the Bakoena, consolidate as small chiefdoms across the territory of present-day Lesotho.
Moshoeshoe I, the future founder of the Basotho nation, is born at Menkhoaneng.
Moshoeshoe I begins uniting scattered Sotho-speaking refugees and clans into a single people in the wake of regional upheaval.
The Difaqane (Lifaqane) wars of mass displacement, triggered by Zulu expansion, scatter peoples across the highveld and drive refugees toward Moshoeshoe.
Moshoeshoe I establishes his stronghold at the mountain fortress of Thaba Bosiu, which becomes the heart of the Basotho kingdom.
French Protestant missionaries of the Paris Evangelical Missionary Society arrive at Moshoeshoe's invitation, founding the Morija mission.
Moshoeshoe I signs a treaty with the British Cape governor seeking protection against encroaching Boer settlers.
Basotho forces repel a British military expedition, reinforcing Moshoeshoe's authority over his expanding kingdom.
The first Free State-Basotho War erupts as Boer settlers and the Basotho clash over land and grazing rights.
The Seqiti War breaks out, with Orange Free State Boers seizing much of Basutoland's fertile western lowlands.
Britain annexes Basutoland as a protectorate at Moshoeshoe's request, halting Boer conquest of the kingdom.
The Treaty of Aliwal North fixes the boundary with the Orange Free State, formalizing the loss of conquered lowland territory.
Moshoeshoe I dies at Thaba Bosiu, leaving a unified Basotho nation under British protection.
Basutoland is annexed to the Cape Colony, placing the Basotho under direct Cape administration.
The Gun War erupts as the Basotho resist Cape Colony attempts to disarm them, asserting their autonomy.
Following the Gun War, Basutoland reverts to direct British rule as a crown territory administered separately from the Cape.
Basutoland remains a British High Commission Territory and is excluded from the newly formed Union of South Africa.
Basutoland contributes troops who serve with Allied forces during the Second World War.
The Basutoland African Congress, later the Basutoland Congress Party, is founded by Ntsu Mokhehle, launching organized nationalist politics.
The Basutoland National Party is established by Chief Leabua Jonathan, becoming a major political force.
A new constitution introduces an elected legislative council, expanding African political participation.
Pre-independence elections give the Basutoland National Party victory, and Chief Leabua Jonathan becomes prime minister.
Basutoland gains full independence as the Kingdom of Lesotho on 4 October, with Moshoeshoe II as king and Leabua Jonathan as prime minister.
Lesotho is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
Prime Minister Leabua Jonathan annuls elections apparently won by the opposition, suspends the constitution, and rules by decree.
A failed uprising by Basutoland Congress Party supporters is crushed, prompting many opposition figures to flee into exile.
Leabua Jonathan is overthrown in a military coup led by Major General Justin Lekhanya amid a South African economic blockade.
King Moshoeshoe II is deposed and exiled by the military, and his son is installed as King Letsie III.
Multiparty democracy is restored as the Basotho Congress Party wins decisive elections, ending military rule.
King Moshoeshoe II is restored to the throne after Letsie III abdicates in his favor.
King Moshoeshoe II dies in a car accident and Letsie III returns to the throne as monarch.
Disputed elections spark unrest, prompting a controversial South African and Botswana SADC military intervention to restore order.
Construction milestones of the Lesotho Highlands Water Project advance, with the Katse Dam beginning to deliver water to South Africa.
Peaceful general elections under a new mixed-member proportional system reaffirm the Lesotho Congress for Democracy in power.
Pakalitha Mosisili's faction forms the Democratic Congress amid party splits, reshaping Lesotho's political landscape.
Tom Thabane forms Lesotho's first coalition government after inconclusive elections, ousting long-serving Mosisili.
An attempted military coup forces Prime Minister Tom Thabane to flee briefly to South Africa before SADC mediation defuses the crisis.
Army commander Maaparankoe Mahao is killed by soldiers, deepening a political and security crisis that draws SADC scrutiny.
Tom Thabane returns as prime minister after coalition elections, while political instability and assassinations continue to trouble the country.
Tom Thabane resigns amid a murder investigation linked to his estranged wife's death, and Moeketsi Majoro becomes prime minister.
Lesotho confirms its first COVID-19 cases, the last country in Africa to report the pandemic's arrival.
Businessman Sam Matekane's new Revolution for Prosperity party wins elections, and he becomes prime minister.
